Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around visual materials like diagrams, charts, and video lectures since that's how I learn best. I would set aside about three hours daily, usually in the evenings after work, to go through course content. I created color-coded notes and mind maps to help me process information, though honestly my routine fell apart as the semester progressed. I had good intentions at the start, but life got complicated and I couldn't maintain the structure I'd built.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. Early on, I tried to stick to regular evening study sessions, but that consistency didn't last. When things got overwhelming, I'd study in bursts—sometimes staying up late before something was due, other times going days without opening my materials. Being 27 and juggling other responsibilities meant I couldn't always prioritize the way I wanted to.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS to check announcements or see if there were new video lectures posted. Sometimes it was peer messages that prompted me—I'd get a notification that someone in my study group had posted something. Other times, honestly, it was anxiety about falling behind that pushed me to log in, even if I didn't end up doing much productive work once I was there.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine, like checking email. I'd log in with my coffee and scan through what was new, watch part of a video, maybe read through discussion posts. That habit formed because I connected with a few peers early on and wanted to stay in the loop with them. But when my assignment completion started slipping, logging in started to feel more stressful than routine.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I'm a visual learner and watching instructors explain concepts with diagrams really helped things click. I also found the discussion forums useful because hearing how other students understood concepts gave me different perspectives. The peer interactions I had, though limited, helped me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials and text-heavy documents because they just didn't stick for me the way visual content did. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely made it through them. Some of the optional quizzes I also avoided because I was scared of confirming how little I actually understood—fear held me back from using tools that might have actually helped me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance to understanding the bigger picture or when it connected concepts visually. If I could create a mind map or diagram from it, I knew it was valuable. Activities that felt like busywork or didn't clearly connect to what I'd be tested on felt like a waste when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already drowning with required work. Required assignments carried the weight of grades and consequences, so they always took priority even when I was struggling to complete them. I think if I'd been in a better position, I would have engaged more with optional content because some of it looked genuinely interesting and helpful.
Q9: Absolutely—I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download videos and watch them offline on my tablet during my commute. I created extensive handwritten notes with diagrams and flowcharts that I'd review repeatedly. I also used YouTube to find alternative explanations of concepts when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd print out slides and annotate them by hand, or I'd be working through problems in a notebook while referencing downloaded materials. Sometimes I'd spend hours creating visual summaries of entire modules, connecting ideas in ways that helped me understand, but none of that showed as platform activity.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main study method. I'd access a video lecture once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline over the following days. I'd pause, rewind, take visual notes, and really process the content—but the LMS only ever saw that single initial access. My real engagement happened away from the platform.
Q12: Around midterm, things got really hard for me. I was scared—genuinely scared—that I was too far behind to catch up. The fear became paralyzing rather than motivating. I had some personal issues come up that I won't go into, but they drained my energy and made it difficult to focus even when I sat down to study.
Q13: Fatigue was a huge factor for me. Working and studying at 27 is different than when you're younger—my energy levels weren't what they used to be. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen, maybe click through a few pages without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain concepts also made me avoid those topics entirely rather than seeking help.
Q14: Many times, honestly. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through content, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I was going through the motions, clicking through slides, letting videos play in the background while I worried about other things. The activity was there, but the meaningful learning wasn't happening in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The fear of doing poorly made me avoid starting—it sounds backwards, but facing the work meant facing how much I didn't understand. I kept telling myself I'd feel more prepared tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel even more behind. My resilience usually kicks in eventually, but this time I waited too long.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense spikes of activity for me. I'd be relatively quiet on the LMS for days, then suddenly log in multiple times as a deadline approached, frantically trying to catch up. The problem was that by then, I'd often missed foundational content, so my last-minute efforts weren't as effective as they should have been.
Q17: When I did receive feedback, it actually motivated me to engage more—knowing someone was paying attention made the work feel more meaningful. But honestly, with my low assignment completion rate, I didn't get much feedback to work with. The feedback I did receive from peers through the peer support intervention helped me feel less isolated in my struggles.
Q18: The course structure with its sequential modules made sense logically, but once I fell behind, it felt impossible to catch up properly. I couldn't just skip ahead because concepts built on each other. The structure that probably helped organized students actually made my situation worse because I felt I had to go back and master everything I'd missed before moving forward.
Q19: The video content and visual resources made engagement easier for someone like me. But the sheer volume of online courses I was enrolled in—19 of them—made it incredibly hard to give any single course the attention it deserved. The course itself wasn't poorly designed, but my situation made it difficult to engage the way the design intended.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions—but my actual assignment submissions were minimal. For me, it was fear and perfectionism combined. I was engaging with content but too scared to submit work that might confirm I wasn't understanding things correctly. The learning felt safer than the assessment.
Q21: That wasn't my experience—I had high LMS activity but poor performance. But I can see how someone who studies efficiently offline, using textbooks or downloaded materials, might not need to be constantly logged in. They might access what they need, learn it thoroughly elsewhere, and perform well without the platform tracking their actual study time.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, frequency of engagement, and participation in discussions—surface-level activity that indicates presence. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, the offline learning, the mental and emotional state of the student. They can't capture whether someone is truly processing information or just clicking through while their mind is elsewhere.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material, feeling curious about it, and actively working to understand rather than just complete tasks. It means showing up mentally, not just logging in. For me, engagement also involves connecting with peers and feeling like part of a learning community. True engagement is when learning feels meaningful rather than obligatory.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my fear—the genuine scared feeling that kept me from submitting work I'd actually completed. It wouldn't show the hours I spent creating visual study materials offline, or the peer support conversations that happened outside the platform. It also wouldn't capture that despite failing, I actually learned a lot; I just couldn't demonstrate it through the formal assessment structure.
